description: Curated internal briefing notes on agentic-system failure modes and evaluation techniques. Synthesized for demonstration; not verbatim paper abstracts.

entries.title: ReAct: interleaving reasoning and acting
entries.summary: ReAct prompts a model to alternate between free-form reasoning traces and concrete tool actions, feeding each observation back into the next reasoning step.
key_points: Reasoning traces let the agent decompose a task before committing to a tool call.
Observations from tools are appended to context, grounding the next decision.
Works best when the tool surface is small and tools have unambiguous descriptions.
failure_modes: Reasoning drift: the trace rationalizes a wrong tool choice instead of correcting it.
Context bloat: long observation histories crowd out the original instruction.
Premature answering before enough evidence is gathered.
entries.source_note: Field summary of the reason-act pattern as deployed in production agents.

entries.title: Self-reflection and verbal reinforcement
entries.summary: A reflection step asks the agent to critique its own draft against the task and evidence, producing a revised answer without any weight updates.
key_points: A separate critique pass catches unsupported claims the drafting pass missed.
Reflection is most effective when the critique is grounded in retrieved evidence, not vibes.
One or two reflection rounds capture most of the gain; more rounds rarely help.
failure_modes: Sycophantic revision: the reviser agrees with the draft instead of challenging it.
Over-editing: a correct answer gets rewritten into a worse one.
Unbounded loops when there is no stop condition on the reflection cycle.
entries.source_note: Notes on verbal self-correction loops for agent answers.

entries.title: Tool-use failure taxonomy
entries.summary: Tool-call failures cluster into selection errors, argument errors, and grounding errors. The dominant production failure is wrong-tool-selection, not malformed arguments.
key_points: Selection errors: the agent picks the wrong tool for the task.
Argument errors: right tool, malformed or hallucinated arguments.
Grounding errors: the agent ignores or misreads the tool's returned observation.
Clear, disjoint tool descriptions reduce selection errors more than any prompt tweak.
failure_modes: Overlapping tool descriptions force the model to guess between near-duplicates.
Silent argument coercion hides bugs until they surface downstream.
Tools that return unstructured blobs invite grounding errors.
entries.source_note: Taxonomy distilled from observed tool-call traces.

entries.title: Hallucinated tool calls
entries.summary: Agents sometimes emit calls to tools that do not exist, or fabricate observations for tools they never actually invoked.
key_points: Fabricated observations are the most dangerous: the agent invents a plausible result.
Constraining the model to a registered tool schema removes nonexistent-tool calls.
Echoing the real observation back into context before answering reduces fabrication.
failure_modes: The agent reports a tool result that was never returned.
Calls to plausibly-named but unregistered tools.
Confident final answers built on imagined evidence.
entries.source_note: Incident notes on fabricated tool activity.

entries.title: Multi-agent coordination and delegation
entries.summary: Hierarchical delegation (a coordinator routing to specialists) outperforms a single monolithic agent when sub-tasks need distinct tools or judgment, at the cost of more orchestration state.
key_points: Explicit sequential or hierarchical control beats free-for-all transfer for predictability.
Shared session state is the contract between agents; keep its keys explicit.
Each hand-off is a place for context to be dropped or duplicated.
failure_modes: State keys written by one agent and silently overwritten by another.
Coordinator loops where two agents transfer back and forth.
Lost context across hand-offs producing inconsistent final answers.

entries.title: LLM-as-judge: reliability and bias
entries.summary: Using an LLM to score another model's output scales evaluation but inherits biases: position bias, verbosity bias, and self-preference. A fixed rubric with per-dimension scores mitigates them.
key_points: Score discrete dimensions (e.g. groundedness, tool-selection, conciseness) rather than one vague number.
Verbosity bias inflates scores for longer answers; conciseness should be scored explicitly.
Held-out reference answers calibrate the judge and expose drift.
failure_modes: Position bias: the first answer shown wins regardless of quality.
Self-preference: a judge favors outputs from its own model family.
Rubric collapse: all dimensions move together because the judge scores a gestalt.
entries.source_note: Reliability notes for automated scoring pipelines.

entries.title: RAG chunking and retrieval grounding
entries.summary: Chunk size and overlap drive retrieval quality more than the choice of vector store. Too-large chunks dilute relevance; too-small chunks fragment the answer's evidence.
key_points: Chunk on semantic boundaries (sections, paragraphs) rather than fixed token windows when possible.
Modest overlap preserves context across chunk edges.
Always carry the source id with each chunk so answers can be grounded and audited.
failure_modes: Oversized chunks bury the relevant sentence among irrelevant text.
Undersized chunks split a fact across two retrievals, neither sufficient alone.
Lost provenance: retrieved text with no source id cannot be verified.
entries.source_note: Retrieval tuning notes for enterprise knowledge bases.

entries.title: Long-horizon credit assignment
entries.summary: In multi-step agent trajectories, a single early mistake can sink an otherwise correct run. Trajectory-level eval (not just final-answer eval) is needed to locate where reliability breaks.
key_points: Score intermediate steps, not only the final answer, to localize failures.
Per-step tracing with stable ids makes failures reproducible and diff-able.
Tool-call accuracy at each step is a leading indicator of end-task success.
failure_modes: Final-answer-only eval hides which step actually failed.
Compounding errors: a small early miss amplifies over a long horizon.
No stable step ids, so failures cannot be traced across runs.
entries.source_note: Notes on evaluating long-horizon tool-using agents.
